title: Testing CSP-strict pages without breaking inline assertions
scenario_context: App enforces strict CSP. Playwright's evaluate() injects scripts that get blocked. Tests start failing with cryptic 'unsafe-eval' errors.
what_should_be_tested: That CSP-strict pages still function correctly under test. Use Playwright APIs that don't require eval, and confirm CSP doesn't break the actual user flows.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Disabling CSP for tests (defeats security review)
Using exposeFunction without understanding CSP boundary
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Playwright's locator-based APIs (getByRole, getAttribute, textContent) don't need eval.
2. evaluate() does need eval — if CSP forbids unsafe-eval, switch to attribute-based assertions.
3. Better: test that CSP is actually enforced by checking response headers, not by working around it.
4. exposeFunction works but creates a tiny script tag — verify CSP allows the test helper origin.
5. Reserve evaluate() for cases that genuinely need DOM measurements.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Tests disable CSP → false confidence the app works under it → CSP violations in prod break inline handlers.

title: Vitest matchers vs Jest matchers — gotchas during migration
scenario_context: Migrating Jest suite to Vitest. Most code works, but tests using `expect.assertions(n)` and `jest.fn().mockName()` produce different behavior. Silent bugs slip in.
what_should_be_tested: That migrated tests preserve their original guarantees. Specifically, async tests still verify all expected assertions ran.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Blind find-replace jest → vi (misses behavior differences)
Removing expect.assertions because Vitest behaves differently
Not running existing tests in Vitest mode before deleting Jest
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. expect.assertions(n) and expect.hasAssertions() work identically in Vitest.
2. jest.fn().mockName() → vi.fn().mockName() — same API.
3. Timers: vi.useFakeTimers default doesn't replace queueMicrotask; Jest 27+ does. Use vi.useFakeTimers({toFake:['setTimeout','queueMicrotask']}).
4. Module mocking: Jest auto-mocks; Vitest requires explicit vi.mock with factory.
5. globals: false (Vitest default) requires explicit imports. globals: true closer to Jest.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Silent test weakening: async tests with forgotten awaits pass instead of fail.
ci_considerations: Codemod is one-time. Run full suite under both Jest and Vitest during migration window.

title: Testing portal-rendered modals
scenario_context: Modal renders via createPortal to document.body. Tests use container-scoped queries (getByRole inside the rendered tree), can't find the modal. Workaround was getElementById — brittle.
what_should_be_tested: Modal opens, has correct content, closes on backdrop click and Escape, traps focus. Queries use screen.* (document-scoped), not container-scoped.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: container-scoped queries when content is in a portal
Asserting modal is in container.children (it's not — it's in document.body)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Portals render outside the React tree. Use `screen` queries which span the whole document.
2. RTL automatically cleans up document.body between tests — portals don't leak.
3. Focus trap: pressing Tab at the last focusable element wraps to first.
4. Escape closes — keyboard accessibility.
5. Backdrop click closes — common pattern but not universal; assert per design intent.
mock_strategy: None — real portal, real keyboard events.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Container queries miss portal — tests pass trivially or skip the modal entirely.

title: Testing CORS preflight handling
scenario_context: API allows specific origins. Bug shipped: a wildcard regex match (`.example.com`) matched `attackerexample.com`. Tests passed because nobody tested malicious origins.
what_should_be_tested: That allowed origins get CORS headers; disallowed origins do NOT. Including attacker-style origins designed to bypass naive regex.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No test for the OPTIONS preflight specifically
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. CORS preflight: OPTIONS request with Origin + Access-Control-Request-Method. Server must return Access-Control-Allow-Origin if allowed.
2. Match origins with an explicit allowlist OR escape regex (`/^https:\/\/(app|admin)\.example\.com$/`).
3. Test malicious origins: subdomain.attacker.com, attackerexample.com, evil.example.com.evil.
4. OPTIONS for disallowed origin: omit ACAO header. Browser blocks. Server still returns 204 (preflight conventions).
5. Test actual GET/POST too — preflight is half the story.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Regex bypass allowed any-origin requests, exposing tokens. Explicit malicious-origin tests catch.

title: Property test for a CSV parser
scenario_context: Custom CSV parser handles quoted fields, escaped quotes, embedded newlines. Bug shipped: a quoted field with comma+quote+newline (`"a,\"b\"\nc"`) parsed as two rows.
what_should_be_tested: Round-trip: encode(decode(csv)) === csv (for canonical CSV). Decoding produces correct shape for arbitrary string inputs with arbitrary embedded specials.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Skipping the 'field contains delimiter' edge case
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. CSV round-trip is the spec. Encode a 2D string array → parse it back → equal.
2. Generate arbitrary rows with arbitrary strings, including commas, quotes, newlines.
3. Field contains comma → must be quoted. Field contains quote → quote doubled. Field contains newline → must be quoted.
4. Edge: empty field, empty row, leading/trailing whitespace preservation.
5. Shrink to minimal failing CSV.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Sales report import broke for any cell with quotes+commas; silent data corruption.

title: Test reporters: integrating with code review tools
scenario_context: Failed CI tests post no signal to PR. Reviewers don't know about failures until pipeline is red 20 min in. Need test failures inline in the PR Files Changed view.
what_should_be_tested: That failing tests annotate the failing line in the PR. Reviewer sees red marker in Files Changed without opening CI logs.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Custom reporters that break when test runner upgrades
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. GitHub's check-annotation API places per-line markers on PR Files Changed view.
2. Vitest's GitHub Actions reporter emits ::error file=...,line=... commands.
3. Annotations show inline in PR — reviewer sees 'test failed because X expected Y'.
4. Limit to changed files to avoid annotation noise from unrelated failures.
5. Same pattern with linters, type errors — unified review experience.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Reviewer merges, CI red, deploy blocked — feedback loop too late. Inline annotations catch reviewer's eye in Files Changed.
ci_considerations: Zero added runtime. Free with built-in reporter.

title: Testing CommonJS interop for libraries with named exports
scenario_context: Project uses ESM. Test imports a CJS-only library; sometimes works, sometimes fails with 'X is not a function'. Behavior differs between dev and test.
what_should_be_tested: That CJS libraries are imported in a way that works across both runtime AND test. Use namespace import or wrapper, document the gotcha.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Named import from CJS (`import {x} from 'lib'`) — works sometimes
Using require() in ESM (loses tree-shaking, runs differently in test)
No test asserting the import works
Mocking the library away instead of fixing the import
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. CJS modules don't expose named exports the same way ESM does. Node's interop is heuristic.
2. Safest: `import lib from 'cjs-lib'; const {x} = lib;` — works everywhere.
3. Vitest's optimizeDeps config pre-bundles deps as ESM for tests, smoothing differences.
4. Test asserts the import behavior — fails immediately if interop breaks.
5. Document in CLAUDE.md or import-style guide.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Named import works in dev (Vite bundles), fails in production runtime. Test catches before merge.
ci_considerations: ~30ms. inline:[] adds small bundle-time cost for tests but eliminates interop surprises.

title: Testing components with React Server Components / async components
scenario_context: Next.js app router uses async Server Components. Standard render() can't render async functions. Tests need a different harness.
what_should_be_tested: That async Server Components render the right output for given props, including data fetching. Use jsdom or node + a renderer that supports async components.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mocking the entire fetch inside the component (couples to impl)
E2E-only coverage (too slow for unit-scale logic)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Async components can't be rendered by standard test renderers. Two options: (a) extract data-fetching logic to plain async functions, test those; (b) use @testing-library/react with React 19's async component support OR react's renderToString.
2. Preferred (a): keep RSCs as thin presentational wrappers; test the pure data + pure render functions.
3. For full RSC: renderToString from react-dom/server, parse output, assert.
4. E2E covers integration; unit covers logic. Keep ratios sane.
5. Pattern: getProductData(id) → product object → ProductView(product). Test getProductData against DB; test ProductView against props.
mock_strategy: DB real for data layer. ProductView pure — pass props.
edge_cases_tested: Async wrapper tested separately via E2E
what_would_break_without_this_test: All logic in async component → only E2E can test → slow + brittle. Splitting enables fast unit tests.

title: Testing analytics events fire without false positives
scenario_context: App fires analytics events (Segment / Mixpanel) on user actions. Bug: an event was renamed but tests still asserted old name. Analytics broken for 2 weeks, dashboards empty.
what_should_be_tested: That specific events fire with specific properties on specific user actions. Use a real analytics SDK with a network-intercepting transport that records calls.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mocking window.analytics directly (the wrapper might break)
Asserting on analytics.track called (doesn't catch property changes)
No assertion on properties (event fires with wrong data)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Intercept the network request the analytics SDK makes. That's what Segment/Mixpanel/etc. ultimately do.
2. Playwright's page.route() captures calls; assertion is on the request body.
3. Test the CRITICAL events (signup, purchase, page-view of paid feature) — not every click.
4. Assert event name + key properties. Don't assert on context.timestamp etc. (volatile).
5. Bug it catches: rename event without updating wherever the dashboard expects it.
mock_strategy: Network interception at the analytics endpoint. Real SDK, real client code path.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Event rename ships → dashboards empty → discovered weeks later. Test fails on rename, forcing dashboard update PR alongside.
ci_considerations: ~3s per test. No external service needed.

title: Testing API request validation with edge-case payloads
scenario_context: POST /api/orders validates payload with Zod. Tests cover valid + obvious-invalid. Bug shipped: extra fields silently accepted, nullable fields set to 'null' string instead of actual null.
what_should_be_tested: That validation rejects malformed inputs precisely: missing required, wrong types, extra fields (strict mode), null vs undefined distinction, oversized payloads.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Loose Zod schemas (.passthrough) that hide field-set bugs
Not testing 'null' string vs actual null
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. .strict() rejects unknown fields. Test sends a payload with an extra field; expects 400.
2. .nullable() vs .optional() are different. Test both.
3. Number coerced from string (`z.coerce.number()`) handles JSON quirks; test it.
4. Payload size limit at express.json({limit:'100kb'}). Test oversized → 413.
5. Error response shape: {error: 'validation', fields: [{path, message}]}. Clients can render field errors.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Extra-field bug let attackers set internal flags. Strict + tests catch.

title: Property test for a JWT verification function
scenario_context: verifyJwt(token,secret) used in auth middleware. Bug: signature check used non-constant-time comparison, vulnerable to timing attacks AND occasionally produced false positives on specially-crafted tokens.
what_should_be_tested: For any valid token, verify succeeds. For any tampered token, verify fails. For invalid format, throws. Across arbitrary payloads.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Tampering only the payload (forgetting signature/header tamper)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Property: sign+verify round-trips. Property: tamper any byte → verify fails.
2. Generate arbitrary payloads; sign them; assert verify returns same payload.
3. Mutate the token (flip a bit anywhere) → verify must fail.
4. Use crypto.timingSafeEqual at the impl level (we can test that the wrapper exists, not the timing itself).
5. Malformed (not three-part) → throws specific error class.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Timing attack vector + edge-case forgery. Properties catch by mutating tokens systematically.

title: Mutation testing reveals weak conditional coverage
scenario_context: hasDiscount(user, item) function. 100% line coverage. Stryker shows 6 survived ConditionalExpression mutations.
what_should_be_tested: Each conditional branch independently. The if-else tree must be exercised for both paths at every node.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single 'happy path' test covering all branches
Tests with overlapping inputs (same condition triggers multiple times)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. ConditionalExpression mutations replace `a && b` with `true && b` and `a && false` etc.
2. Each branch needs at least one test where the branch matters (changes outcome).
3. Truth table: enumerate conditions, write one test per row.
4. Branch coverage in vitest catches partial coverage that lines miss.
5. Pattern: when conditional grows complex, extract pure predicate functions and test them in isolation.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Surviving mutants = real branches no test exercises. Truth-table approach systematically kills them.

title: Test data builders with chainable fluent API
scenario_context: Factory-based seeds work but builder pattern reads better for complex objects. `aUser().withTier('pro').withAdmin().build()` self-documents the test intent.
what_should_be_tested: That builders produce schema-valid output, support composition, and remain in sync with schema changes.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Too many builder methods (drift from real domain)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Builder is immutable: each `with*` returns new builder. Tests can't accidentally mutate.
2. .build() runs schema parse — fails fast if combination is invalid.
3. Compose builders: `aUser().withOrder(anOrder().withItem(...).build()).build()`.
4. Builder defaults reflect 'typical valid', not 'tricky edge case' — tests opt into edge cases.
5. Pattern shines for test readability when objects have 10+ fields.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Mutable builders leak state across tests. Schema-validated build catches drift.

title: Testing IntersectionObserver-based lazy load
scenario_context: Image lazy-loads via IntersectionObserver. jsdom doesn't implement IO. Tests crash. Workarounds use polyfill or mock — but bug shipped when threshold changed and visibility didn't trigger load.
what_should_be_tested: That intersecting triggers load, non-intersecting doesn't. Mock IO programmatically to invoke entry callbacks.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Polyfill that doesn't match real IO semantics
Mocking the whole IO without an invocation API
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Provide a controllable IO mock that exposes a 'trigger(entries)' method.
2. Test asserts: before trigger, img.src is placeholder; after trigger with isIntersecting:true, img.src is real URL.
3. Threshold matters: test entry with isIntersecting:false (below threshold) — no load.
4. Cleanup: unmount disconnects the observer (no leak).
mock_strategy: Custom IO mock with explicit trigger API. Controllable, no polyfill mismatch.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Threshold bug ships, images don't load until scrolled fully into view. Trigger-based test catches.

title: Testing print stylesheets
scenario_context: Invoice page has a print view. Designer changed print CSS, sidebar still printed because @media print rule was overridden. Discovered when accounting printed 500 invoices.
what_should_be_tested: That print CSS hides what it should and shows what it should. Use Playwright's emulateMedia to render as print, screenshot, assert.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No automated coverage of print CSS
Asserting on screen DOM, expecting print rules to apply
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. page.emulateMedia({media:'print'}) switches the media context. CSS @media print rules apply.
2. Assert visibility via toBeVisible() — Playwright respects display:none.
3. Screenshot for full visual regression of print layout.
4. Test specifically what should be hidden (nav, sidebar) and what should be present (logo, footer).
what_would_break_without_this_test: Print CSS regression goes unnoticed until physical printing.

title: Custom Vitest reporter for domain-specific output
scenario_context: CI shows generic test names. Team wants test output grouped by feature area with custom severity tagging.
what_should_be_tested: That a custom reporter produces team-friendly output without breaking standard tooling.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Reporter that throws on edge cases (crashes runner)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Run alongside default reporters — additive, not replacement.
2. Reporter receives lifecycle events: onTestModuleEnd, onTestCaseResult.
3. Group by file path → feature (e.g., src/billing/* → 'Billing').
4. Output: summary by feature with pass/fail counts.
5. Keep IO bounded — write once at end, not per test.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Generic output hides which area regressed. Per-feature summary surfaces hotspots.
ci_considerations: Zero runtime cost. File output uploaded as artifact.

title: Testing SSE endpoints (server-sent events)
scenario_context: GET /api/events streams server-sent events. supertest doesn't handle streaming. Tests skip the streaming path; bug shipped where event delimiter was wrong char.
what_should_be_tested: That events are emitted, formatted correctly per SSE spec, and the connection closes cleanly. Use Node http client + manual parsing.
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. SSE format: `data: <payload>\n\n`. Test that exact format reaches the client.
2. Use native fetch with ReadableStream reader, OR EventSource polyfill in test.
3. Server should respect Last-Event-Id header for resume.
4. Connection keepalive: heartbeat comment `:hb\n\n` every N seconds prevents proxy idle-close.
5. Test client disconnect → server stops emitting (cleanup).
mock_strategy: Real server, real network, real SSE parsing.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Wrong delimiter → client never parses event. Format test catches in CI.

title: Testing animation completion / Framer Motion
scenario_context: Modal slide-in animation. Tests don't wait for animation, click happens before button is interactive. Flaky failures.
what_should_be_tested: That animation completes before subsequent interactions are expected to work. Or, that animations are disabled in test environment for determinism.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Disabling animations selectively (some pass, some fail)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Reduced motion: app honors prefers-reduced-motion. Test env sets it always → animations skip.
2. Framer Motion: <MotionConfig reducedMotion="always"> wraps tests. Animations resolve immediately.
3. For tests that NEED to assert animation behavior, use animation.completion promises.
4. CSS transitions: globally disabled via injected stylesheet.
5. Pattern: production = animated, test = instant. No timing flakes.
mock_strategy: CSS injection + matchMedia stub. Real Framer Motion respects reduced-motion.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Flaky CI: click before animation completes → action lost. Disabled animations = zero flake.
ci_considerations: Setup once at vitest startup. Faster tests overall.

title: Property test for a permission/role check function
scenario_context: can(user, action, resource) returns boolean. Critical security boundary. Example tests pass; production: admin accidentally lost ability to delete because role hierarchy had a typo.
what_should_be_tested: Invariants: admin ⊇ manager ⊇ member. If admin can do X, manager can do X (for delegated ops). For any user, can(...) is deterministic for same inputs.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Per-action tests that drift from intended hierarchy
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Role hierarchy is the spec: admin ⊇ manager ⊇ member ⊇ guest.
2. Property: for any action a member can do, manager and admin can do it too.
3. Property: for any action admin CAN'T do (e.g., 'become user'), no role can do it.
4. Deterministic: same inputs → same output. Catch hidden state.
5. Generate arbitrary roles and actions; assert the partial order holds.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Privilege drop bug: admin lost delete when matrix was reordered. Monotonicity property catches.

title: Test results history and trend tracking
scenario_context: Suite passes today, fails tomorrow, passes the day after — same code. No history, can't see whether 'flaky' tests have been flaky for weeks vs days.
what_should_be_tested: Trends over time: which tests historically flake, which are slowing down, which were recently added.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No history — every CI run is isolated
Storing history in CI logs (rotated away)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Push test results to a time-series store (or git-tracked JSON) after each CI run.
2. Compute per-test pass rate over last N runs.
3. Alert when a test crosses thresholds: pass rate <95%, runtime up 30% vs 7-day median.
4. Dashboard: top 10 slowest, top 10 flakiest.
5. Self-healing: tests that pass 100% for 30 days drop off the watch list.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Flakiness invisible, performance drift invisible. History surfaces both.
ci_considerations: ~1s per run. History stored in repo or S3.

title: Decision: when E2E is the only way
scenario_context: Team debates 'should X be E2E or integration?' for every test. No rubric → inconsistent decisions → suite drifts.
what_should_be_tested: A documented rubric for choosing test type. Apply per-feature for clarity.
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. E2E justified when: real browser interaction matters (drag, copy/paste), multi-service flow, third-party JS integration (Stripe Elements), responsive layout behavior.
2. Integration: API-DB boundary, multi-table consistency, transaction semantics.
3. Unit: pure logic, single function, deterministic.
4. Rubric goes in CLAUDE.md so anyone making a test choice references it.
5. Periodic audit: are E2E tests testing things only E2E can verify?
solution:
// docs/TEST_TYPE_RUBRIC.md
/*
# Choosing the right test type

## E2E (Playwright) — only when ALL of:
- Real browser semantics required (drag, copy/paste, focus management across iframes)
- OR multi-page flow with real navigation
- OR third-party JS integration (Stripe Elements, OAuth popup, embedded video player)
- OR responsive/visual behavior under different viewports

## Integration (Vitest + real DB/Redis) — when:
- DB transaction semantics matter (rollback, isolation)
- Multiple modules collaborate via real infrastructure
- API contract validation
- Middleware chain behavior

## Unit (Vitest, no infra) — when:
- Pure function or single class
- Logic doesn't touch IO
- Behavior fully deterministic

## Red flags pushing UP the pyramid:
- E2E to test a math function → move to unit
- E2E to test a server endpoint → move to integration
- E2E to test 5 form-field combinations → 1 E2E + 4 unit/integration

## Red flags pushing DOWN:
- Unit that mocks 8 things → integration
- Integration with frontend rendering that should be E2E → E2E

## Rule of thumb
The cheapest test that EXERCISES THE REAL BUG SURFACE wins.
*/

// scripts/audit-test-types.ts — flag suspiciously-shaped tests
import {glob} from 'glob';
import {readFileSync} from 'node:fs';
for(const f of await glob('tests/e2e/**/*.spec.ts')){
  const src=readFileSync(f,'utf8');
  if(!src.match(/page\.|browser\./))console.warn(`${f}: E2E test with no page/browser interaction — should it be integration?`);
  if((src.match(/expect\(/g)?.length??0)<2)console.warn(`${f}: E2E test with <2 assertions — likely test smell`);
}
what_would_break_without_this_test: Suite drifts toward whatever pattern was easiest. Rubric + audit keeps pyramid sane.
ci_considerations: Audit runs weekly. Doc reviewed quarterly.

title: Testing with real auth provider (Auth0 / Clerk) in E2E
scenario_context: App uses Auth0. E2E test 'signs in' via filling Auth0's hosted page. Tests break on every Auth0 UI change. 6 incidents in 1 year.
what_should_be_tested: Auth integration without filling the third-party UI. Use Auth0's API to programmatically obtain a session token, set it as a cookie/storage, navigate as authenticated user.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Filling Auth0/Clerk hosted form via Playwright (brittle)
Disabling auth in E2E (loses real session validation)
Single shared test account (rate limits + collisions)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Auth0 has a Resource Owner Password Grant API for trusted environments. Obtain a token directly.
2. Inject token into browser as auth cookie/session via context.addCookies.
3. Per-test or per-suite test users via Management API.
4. Auth flow itself (login form) tested ONCE in a focused spec.
5. Same pattern: Clerk, Firebase, Cognito — each has a programmatic token endpoint.
mock_strategy: Real Auth0 for token. Browser injection bypasses the UI.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Auth0 UI redesign breaks 50 tests. Programmatic approach is immune.
ci_considerations: ~2s setup once. Form spec ~5s, runs once.

title: Testing optimistic locking / version-based concurrency
scenario_context: Document edit uses version field for optimistic concurrency. Two users edit simultaneously; second save should fail with conflict. Not tested; bug shipped where version compare was string instead of number, always 'matched'.
what_should_be_tested: That concurrent saves: first wins, second gets 409 conflict. Server reads version, compares, updates only if match.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Sequential test claiming to test concurrency
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Concurrency test: both writers obtain version V; write with V; exactly one succeeds.
2. Promise.all to simulate parallel — DB row-level lock determines winner.
3. Loser gets 409 with current version → client refetches and retries.
4. Version is integer monotonic. Test that successful save increments version.
5. Real bug: comparing JSON-stringified version (string) vs DB integer — always unequal, all saves rejected.
mock_strategy: Real DB; concurrency exercised via Promise.all.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Type mismatch in version compare: all saves silently dropped. Real concurrent test catches it.

title: Inline snapshots for compact tests
scenario_context: Each .snap file requires opening another file to review. Tests with many small snapshots become hard to read.
what_should_be_tested: Same coverage but with inline snapshots co-located with assertions.
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Inline: snapshot lives in the test file. Reviewer sees expected next to assertion.
2. Use for small (<10 lines) structured outputs.
3. External for larger trees that would dwarf the test code.
4. Updates: --update flag still works for inline.
5. Pairs well with serializers (test-042).
what_would_break_without_this_test: Reviewer skips opening .snap files; snapshot drift unnoticed.

title: Testing optimistic updates that need rollback
scenario_context: Like button: optimistic increment on click, rollback on server error. Test asserts the success path; rollback never tested. Bug: rollback logic decremented to negative when retry queued the increment again.
what_should_be_tested: On success: count increments visibly, settles to server count. On failure: count reverts to original, error visible.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No assertion on final state after rollback
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Optimistic: UI updates immediately; server call in flight. Two paths to verify.
2. Failure mock: server returns 500; UI should revert.
3. Concurrent clicks during failure: counts must stay consistent.
4. Use MSW or vi.fn mock for the request.
5. Assert final state after server resolves, not just immediate.
mock_strategy: onLike is vi.fn() with controlled resolve/reject.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Rollback logic bug ships, like counts drift negative.

title: Testing internationalization (i18n)
scenario_context: App supports en, es, fr, ja. Tests in English only. Spanish-specific bug shipped: pluralization rule wrong, '0 items' became '0 elementos' instead of 'ningún elemento'.
what_should_be_tested: Per-locale rendering of critical strings: dates, numbers, plurals. At least smoke per supported locale.
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Locale via app's locale switcher OR header (Accept-Language).
2. Assert on FORMAT, not exact string: `expect(date).toMatch(/^\d+ [a-zA-Z]+ \d{4}$/)` for English.
3. For specific strings (button labels), maintain a per-locale fixture file translated alongside the strings.
4. Critical-path smoke per locale: home renders, can navigate, can submit form.
5. Don't translate every test — overkill; smoke per locale is the right balance.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Locale bugs ship to non-English users, missed by English-only tests.

title: Testing idempotency keys for write operations
scenario_context: POST /api/payments accepts Idempotency-Key header. Client retries on network blip; server should return same response, not double-charge. Tests cover happy path; retry semantics untested.
what_should_be_tested: Same key + same body returns same response. Same key + different body returns 422 (key already used with different request). Different key creates new resource.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mocking the idempotency store (then it's not really tested)
No test for the conflict case
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Idempotency: store key → response in Redis with TTL.
2. Subsequent request with same key returns cached response.
3. Different body with same key → 422 — protects against bug-in-client sending mismatched payloads.
4. TTL test: after expiry, key reusable (separate test).
5. Atomic write of key+response — race conditions on first call resolved by SETNX.
mock_strategy: Real Redis (Testcontainers); fresh DB per test.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Double-charges on retry, or false-rejection of legit retries.

title: Property: feature flag toggle never changes determinism
scenario_context: Feature flag system. Same user, same flag, same context should ALWAYS evaluate the same — otherwise A/B tests break. Bug: a non-deterministic random call leaked into hash, causing flip-flopping.
what_should_be_tested: For any (userId, flagKey, context), repeated evaluation returns the same value within a stable bucket assignment.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Context that includes Date.now (changes per call)
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Flag eval is a pure function of (userId, flagKey, percentage, salt).
2. Determinism: f(x) === f(x) for any x. Repeated calls identical.
3. Stability: percentage rollout monotonic — increasing pct never turns users OFF who were ON.
4. Distribution: ~N% of random userIds evaluate to true at pct=N. Statistical, with tolerance.
5. Hashing: use a deterministic hash (md5/sha) of userId+flagKey. Bucket 0-99 = mod 100.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Flag flickering breaks user experience and A/B integrity.

title: Testing drag-and-drop list reorder
scenario_context: dnd-kit list reorder. Tests skip drag interactions; bug: items lost focus after reorder, breaking keyboard users.
what_should_be_tested: Items can be reordered via keyboard (Space+arrows per dnd-kit pattern). Focus stays on the moved item. Order persists.
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. dnd-kit supports keyboard: Space picks up, arrows move, Space drops.
2. Test via userEvent.keyboard sequences.
3. After reorder, assert getAllByRole('listitem') order, and document.activeElement is the moved item.
4. Mouse drag uses dispatchEvent for mousemove/mouseup — also testable.
5. Catch the focus-loss bug: explicit assert that focused element is the dragged item.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Mouse tests pass, keyboard users locked out.

title: Test environment parity with production
scenario_context: Tests pass locally with Node 22, prod runs Node 20. Test uses a feature not in 20. Hot patches in prod.
what_should_be_tested: Same Node version, same package manager, same env vars set, same OS in CI as in production.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No CI matrix on Node version
test_strategy_reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Pin Node version in package.json engines + .nvmrc + CI workflow.
2. Use lockfile (pnpm-lock.yaml) and require frozen install.
3. CI matrix optionally tests on prod version + next LTS for forward compatibility.
4. Container CI runs on Linux (matches prod image).
5. Env var schema: define required + optional + defaults in one place.
what_would_break_without_this_test: Version drift bugs ship; env-var typos crash prod.
ci_considerations: Engines mismatch fails install. Env schema parse fails app startup with clear message.
